behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real recursive filesystem watching: create a real subdirectory/file under the watched root and verify the running daemon receives and processes it. A mock watcher can hide OS/backend recursion gaps. Rejected higher levels because behavior text is only filesystem watching (no AI/ML output, no multi-agent sequence).
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create `root/sub/a.txt`, and assert exactly one output artifact is produced for that file path within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is path+time-window state logic in the deduper itself; no real OS/network/subprocess is required. Concrete disqualifier for higher levels: behavior text is about "same file path within a configurable time window," not about watcher backend behavior or real infrastructure side effects.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate deduper with window `W`, submit same normalized path twice at `t` and `t+delta<W`, assert second request is rejected; then at `t+delta>W` assert acceptance.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly names OS-emitted duplicate events, which are real watcher/backend phenomena. In-process fakes can miss timing/coalescing patterns and give false confidence. Rejected higher levels because behavior text contains no AI/ML dependency and no chained agentic decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real watcher, perform one real file write pattern that triggers duplicate native events (e.g., write+rename/flush), and assert only one pipeline execution/output occurs for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Directory delete/recreate changes real filesystem identity and watcher subscriptions; this failure appears at OS watcher boundary. A mocked watcher may not reproduce inode/handle invalidation. Rejected higher levels because behavior text is purely daemon+filesystem recovery (no model output, no multi-agent pipeline).
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on real temp dir, delete watched dir, recreate it with same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on interaction of concurrent arrival events plus filesystem timestamp retrieval/precision, which can differ in real OS/filesystems. Mocked events/timestamps can hide true ordering bugs. Rejected higher levels because behavior text is about file ordering only (no AI/ML or multi-agent sequencing).
behaviors.test_description: In a real watched directory, create multiple files nearly simultaneously with controlled creation times, then assert daemon processing order matches observed creation timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a partial-write race involving real filesystem write visibility and event timing; in-process fakes usually cannot reproduce the same boundary conditions. Rejected higher levels because behavior text contains no AI/ML-dependent correctness or chained agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, begin a slow/streaming write to a file in watched dir, verify no processing starts before final close/flush, then verify processing starts after write completion.
